faqs.question: What is Fivetran used for?
faqs.answer: Fivetran is used to automatically extract data from SaaS applications, databases and other sources and load it into a cloud data warehouse or lakehouse like Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift or Databricks, so analytics and data teams have a continuously synced, centralized dataset for reporting, BI and AI workloads without building custom pipelines.

faqs.question: How does Fivetran's pricing work?
faqs.answer: Fivetran uses a usage-based pricing model built around Monthly Active Rows (MAR), the number of unique rows inserted, updated or deleted by a connector each month. A Free plan covers up to 500,000 MAR; paid Standard, Enterprise and Business Critical plans apply a small base fee per connection plus tiered MAR consumption pricing that decreases at higher volumes.

faqs.question: What data warehouses does Fivetran support as destinations?

faqs.question: Did Fivetran acquire HVR?

faqs.question: Did Fivetran merge with dbt Labs?

enrichment.short_description: Flagsmith is an open-source feature flagging, remote configuration, and A/B testing platform that lets engineering teams control feature rollouts across web, mobile, and server-side applications without redeploying code.
enrichment.seo_title: Flagsmith Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Flagsmith review: open-source feature flags, remote config and A/B testing with self-hosting. See 2026 pricing, features, pros, cons and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Flagsmith is an open-source feature flag management, remote configuration, and A/B/multivariate testing platform designed to let software teams control the rollout of new functionality without redeploying code. At its core, Flagsmith lets developers wrap any piece of application logic in a feature flag, then toggle that flag on or off, target it at specific user segments, or gradually roll it out as a percentage of traffic, all from a central dashboard rather than through a new code deployment. Beyond simple on/off toggles, Flagsmith supports remote config values (strings, numbers, and JSON payloads attached to a flag) so teams can change application behavior, copy, or configuration in real time. It also includes native support for A/B testing and multivariate testing, allowing teams to run controlled experiments by serving different flag variations to different user cohorts and measuring the impact on business metrics.

The company behind Flagsmith was founded in London in 2018 by Ben Rometsch and Kyle Johnson, two engineers with a long working history together dating back to Solid State Group, a London-based product engineering agency Rometsch co-founded in 2002 and where Johnson had one of his first professional roles. While building client projects at the agency, Johnson developed an internal feature-flagging tool to let the agency's developers ship incremental changes to client applications safely; that internal tool was originally named Bullet Train, and the legal entity behind the product, Bullet Train Ltd, still carries that name today. The founders later rebranded the product from Bullet Train to Flagsmith, a decision driven largely by search and branding considerations: a name like 'Bullet Train' returned search results dominated by literal trains rather than software, which hurt organic discoverability, whereas 'Flagsmith' clearly signaled the product's purpose as a feature-flag tool and better supported the company's SEO and content-led growth strategy. Flagsmith has been notably vocal about being a bootstrapped, commercial open-source business. The founders have publicly stated they deliberately avoided raising a traditional venture-backed Series A, choosing instead to grow the company using its own revenue and a modest amount of angel investment; total disclosed early funding is estimated in the range of roughly $130,000 to $230,000 depending on the data source (Crunchbase and PitchBook), figures that are small relative to well-funded competitors in the category. According to statements from co-founder Ben Rometsch, Flagsmith reached roughly $1.5 million in annual recurring revenue while remaining profitable, scaling its infrastructure to handle billions of monthly flag-evaluation requests with a lean team of roughly a dozen to two dozen employees spread across multiple continents. The company frames this bootstrapped path as a deliberate values choice: prioritizing customer needs and sustainable, profitable growth over external revenue targets or rapid fundraising-driven expansion, and as a natural extension of their commitment to open source.

Technically, Flagsmith is built as an open-source core (the server-side API and dashboard are released under a permissive license, primarily BSD-3-Clause, with some components under MIT) with a commercial hosted SaaS offering layered on top. The GitHub repository for the core project has accumulated several thousand stars and is composed primarily of Python (powering the Django-based backend API) and TypeScript/JavaScript (powering the React-based admin dashboard and SDKs). Flagsmith ships official client and server-side SDKs for more than a dozen languages and frameworks, including JavaScript, TypeScript, React, Next.js, Python, Node.js, Java, .NET/C#, Ruby, PHP, Go, Rust, Swift/iOS, Kotlin/Android, Flutter, and Elixir, among others, giving it broad coverage across web, mobile, and backend environments. For self-hosting, Flagsmith provides an official Docker Compose configuration that spins up the API, dashboard, and supporting Postgres database with a single command, and it also supports deployment to Kubernetes via Helm charts for teams that need to run the platform inside their own infrastructure for compliance, data residency, latency, or cost reasons. This self-hosting option is a major differentiator relative to closed-source SaaS-only competitors, since it lets regulated industries, government contractors, and privacy-sensitive organizations keep all flag evaluation and user data inside their own network perimeter while still benefiting from Flagsmith's dashboard and workflow tooling. The platform's flag evaluation architecture is designed to be low-latency, with SDKs able to fetch and cache flag states locally so that flag checks in application code do not require a network round trip on every evaluation.

Flagsmith's pricing follows a classic freemium SaaS-plus-self-host model. The Free plan supports up to 50,000 API requests per month, a single team member, and unlimited feature flags, environments, identities, and segments, making it usable for solo developers, side projects, and early-stage startups evaluating the tool. The Start-Up plan is priced at $40 per month when billed yearly (or $45 per month billed monthly) and raises the request allowance to 1,000,000 per month while adding three team members, unlimited projects, scheduled flags, two-factor authentication, A/B and multivariate testing, third-party integrations, and email technical support. The Scale-Up plan is priced at $250 per month billed yearly (or $300 per month billed monthly), supports 5,000,000-plus requests per month, includes five team members with the option to add more at $50 per member per month up to 20, and adds SAML/SSO authentication, governance features such as roles, permissions, change requests, and audit logs, plus priority email and chat support. At the top of the pricing ladder sits a custom-quoted Enterprise plan aimed at organizations needing 5,000,000-plus requests, 20-plus team members, LDAP and SCIM support, tailored hosting arrangements (public cloud, private cloud, or fully self-hosted), onboarding and training, and an enterprise SLA with priority real-time support. Overage beyond a plan's included request volume is billed at roughly $50 per additional million requests, with the effective rate decreasing at higher volumes. Because the core platform is open source, technically sophisticated teams can also self-host the software entirely for free, subject to their own infrastructure costs, which is a meaningful option not available with most closed-source competitors.

Flagsmith's target audience spans individual developers and small startups using the generous free tier for side projects, through growth-stage SaaS companies adopting the Start-Up or Scale-Up plans to manage progressive rollouts and reduce deployment risk, up to larger and regulated enterprises that need the self-hosted or private-cloud enterprise tier for compliance and data sovereignty reasons. In the broader feature management and experimentation category, Flagsmith competes most directly with LaunchDarkly, the market-leading closed-source feature flag platform known for sophisticated targeting, governance, and compliance tooling aimed at large enterprises; Split.io (now part of Harness), which historically paired flags with statistical experimentation; Unleash, another open-source feature flag platform with strong self-hosting credentials that has become popular with large enterprises such as Visa and Samsung; ConfigCat, a lighter-weight remote-config-focused competitor; and PostHog, which bundles feature flags together with product analytics, session replay, and experimentation into a single all-in-one platform rather than a standalone flag tool. Flagsmith differentiates itself primarily on deployment flexibility, offering hosted SaaS, private cloud, and fully self-hosted options side by side, whereas many competitors are SaaS-only or reserve self-hosting exclusively for the highest enterprise tier. It also differentiates on transparent, comparatively affordable mid-market pricing and a genuinely open-source core, in contrast to LaunchDarkly's closed-source, enterprise-oriented model and higher price points. Unlike PostHog, Flagsmith does not bundle native product analytics or session replay, and unlike LaunchDarkly or dedicated experimentation platforms, it lacks built-in advanced statistical significance engines for large-scale experimentation programs, positioning it more as a focused, flexible feature-management platform than an all-in-one product analytics suite. Flagsmith's evolution has consistently tracked toward broadening SDK language coverage, deepening enterprise governance features such as audit logs and role-based access control, and reinforcing its identity as one of the most prominent bootstrapped, profitable, commercial open-source companies in the developer-tools space, a narrative its founders actively promote through public writing, podcasts, and conference talks about building outside the venture capital funding model.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Flagsmith is an open-source feature flagging, remote configuration, and A/B testing platform that helps engineering teams decouple deployment from release. Instead of shipping new code and hoping it works, teams wrap functionality in a flag and control who sees it, in real time, from a central dashboard.

Founded in London in 2018 by Ben Rometsch and Kyle Johnson, Flagsmith grew out of an internal tool called Bullet Train that the pair built while running a product engineering agency. The product was later rebranded to Flagsmith for clearer positioning and better search visibility, and the company has remained bootstrapped ever since, reaching roughly $1.5 million in annual recurring revenue without a venture-backed Series A.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The platform's core capability is feature flag management: unlimited flags, environments, identities, and user segments are available even on the free tier, letting teams gate functionality by user, cohort, or percentage rollout without a code deploy.

A defining feature is deployment flexibility: Flagsmith is available as fully managed SaaS, private cloud, or self-hosted via Docker Compose or Kubernetes/Helm, which lets regulated or privacy-sensitive teams run the entire platform inside their own infrastructure.

The Scale-Up plan is $250/month (billed yearly) for 5,000,000-plus requests, SSO, and governance features like audit logs and change requests, with additional seats at $50/member/month. enrichment.seo_title: Flarum Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Flarum is a free, open-source PHP and Mithril.js forum platform. Explore its features, self-hosting requirements, extensions, pricing, and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Flarum is a free, open-source discussion forum platform designed to power community and support forums for websites. Technically, it pairs a PHP backend, built on Laravel components, with a JavaScript single-page application frontend written in Mithril.js, so that browsing threads, posting replies, and navigating categories happen without full page reloads. The project is released under the MIT license, meaning the core software can be downloaded, self-hosted, modified, and redistributed at no cost. Flarum installs via Composer, PHP's dependency manager, and its own extensions and themes are also distributed as Composer packages, which gives the platform a plugin architecture similar in spirit to WordPress plugins but built around PHP service providers and a documented Extension API rather than simple hooks.

The project's roots trace back further than its public launch. Flarum's creator, Toby Zerner, conceived the underlying design philosophy around 2010 and built early prototypes by 2012, entering the concept into the University of Adelaide's eChallenge programme, where it won second place. Flarum is frequently described as growing out of the lineage of two earlier PHP forum projects, FluxBB and esoTalk, reflecting the backgrounds of its two principal early developers: Zerner, who had worked on esoTalk, and Franz Liedke, the creator of FluxBB. In October 2014, Zerner and Stephen Grace launched a Kickstarter campaign to fund Flarum's development. That campaign was canceled just weeks later, on December 20, 2014, when Zerner instead chose to open-source the project immediately and make the code freely available on GitHub rather than gating it behind crowdfunding. Franz Liedke joined the effort in March 2015, bringing his FluxBB experience to bear on rebuilding the backend around Laravel.

Flarum's development history is unusually long for a project of its kind. The first public beta was released on August 27, 2015, and the frontend was originally built with Ember.js before being rewritten in the lighter-weight Mithril.js framework. From there, the project moved through roughly sixteen beta iterations spanning several years of public testing before reaching its first stable 1.0.0 release on May 27, 2021, more than a decade after Zerner's original 2010 concept and about six and a half years after the project's public open-source debut. That extended beta period was a frequent topic of discussion within the Flarum community itself, with users repeatedly asking when a stable release would arrive as the software matured through real-world use on production forums well before it was formally tagged 1.0.

Leadership of the project changed hands during this period. Toby Zerner announced on July 4, 2019 that he was stepping back from Flarum to focus on a separate premium forum software venture of his own, handing the project's direction to Franz Liedke and Daniel Klabbers. Liedke himself later stepped back from day-to-day leadership in February 2021, citing an inability to commit consistent time, leaving Klabbers as the primary figure guiding ongoing development. To give the project a stable institutional home independent of any single contributor, the remaining team established the Flarum Foundation (registered in the Netherlands as Stichting Flarum), a nonprofit entity that now holds the Flarum trademark and serves as the legal owner of the open-source project, ensuring that governance and intellectual property do not depend on any one person's continued involvement.

Architecturally, Flarum's backend is organized as a modular PHP application; core functionality (discussions, users, tags, notifications, search) ships as first-party 'core' code, while nearly everything else, including many features other forum software would consider standard, is implemented as an extension. Officially bundled extensions handle things like tags, mentions, subscriptions, and flagging, while a large body of community-built extensions, distributed for free on GitHub and Packagist, adds capabilities such as social login, advanced moderation tools, private messaging, custom badges, SEO enhancements, and integrations with external services. Flarum also runs an official premium extensions marketplace on its own website, where independent developers, who must be invited after a review process, can sell more advanced extensions either as one-off purchases or subscriptions directly to forum owners, with a small platform fee funding the marketplace's operations. As of mid-2026, Flarum's actively developed 2.x line requires PHP 8.3 or newer, reflecting the project's ongoing modernization of its dependency baseline, while the long-lived 1.x branch (culminating around version 1.8.x) remains widely deployed on older PHP 7.3-plus environments. The project also maintains a formal security-disclosure process through the huntr.dev bug-bounty and reporting platform, and past releases such as 1.0.2 have shipped fixes for reported issues, including a cross-site scripting vulnerability in the search field, reflecting an ongoing maintenance cadence typical of an actively used web application rather than an abandoned side project.

On pricing, Flarum itself has never operated an official paid cloud-hosting product; an early plan to pair the Kickstarter campaign with a hosted offering was dropped within weeks of the 2014 launch in favor of a purely open-source distribution model. The core software remains entirely free to download and self-host indefinitely, with no feature gating in the free tier. Site owners who prefer not to manage their own servers can instead choose from a number of independent, third-party managed-hosting providers, such as Elestio, 20i, TMDHosting, and 365i, which package Flarum installation, updates, backups, SSL, and monitoring into monthly hosting fees that are set by those providers rather than by the Flarum project. The only money that flows to the Flarum ecosystem directly is through the optional premium extensions marketplace described above, which forum owners can use selectively without being required to purchase anything to run a functional forum.

Flarum's target users are website and community owners who want a lightweight, modern, mobile-friendly discussion forum without the overhead of older PHP forum software or the resource requirements of some newer alternatives. Adopters cited by the project include the mobile network giffgaff, which runs its large peer-support community on Flarum, along with other enterprise and hobbyist communities. Its natural competitors include Discourse, a Ruby on Rails-based forum platform that is open-source but generally heavier to self-host and often consumed as a paid hosted service; phpBB, an older and more traditional PHP forum package with a full-page-reload interface and a large legacy extension library; vBulletin and XenForo, both long-standing commercial, closed-source forum platforms sold under paid licenses; and NodeBB, a Node.js and MongoDB-based forum system with a similarly modern single-page interface. Flarum differentiates itself from these primarily through its combination of a fully free MIT-licensed core (unlike vBulletin and XenForo), a notably lighter and faster single-page-application feel than phpBB or Discourse tend to offer on comparable hardware, and a minimalist, extension-driven design philosophy that keeps the base installation intentionally small so that site owners only add the complexity their community actually needs.

As a product, Flarum continues to evolve under Foundation stewardship, with the 2.0 release moving through release-candidate versions in 2026 after a long beta cycle, mirroring the extended incubation that preceded the original 1.0 release. The project's story, spanning a 2010 concept, a canceled 2014 Kickstarter, a multi-year public beta, a 2021 stable release, and a subsequent transition to nonprofit governance, is unusually well documented for an open-source forum project, giving prospective adopters a clear picture of both its technical maturity and its long-term institutional stability.

From a market-position standpoint, Flarum occupies a specific niche within the broader community-software landscape: it targets teams and individuals who want the modern, app-like feel of a platform like Discourse but without Discourse's typical resource footprint or its tendency to be consumed as a paid hosted product, while also wanting a lighter, more contemporary alternative to legacy PHP forum software such as phpBB, SMF, or vBulletin that many long-running communities were originally built on. Because Flarum is bundled as a minimal core with functionality layered on through extensions, forum administrators retain a high degree of control over what their installation actually does, which appeals to developers and technically capable site owners more than to non-technical users seeking a fully turnkey, one-click hosted product. This trade-off, more configuration effort in exchange for a smaller, faster, and entirely free base platform, has remained consistent throughout Flarum's evolution from its 2014 open-source debut through its 2021 stable release and into its current 2.x development cycle, and it continues to define who chooses Flarum over its commercial and open-source alternatives.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Flarum is a free, open-source discussion forum platform built with a PHP and Laravel-based backend and a Mithril.js single-page application frontend, released under the MIT license. It is designed to be installed on a site owner's own server using Composer, PHP's package manager, and is aimed at communities that want a fast, modern forum without the overhead of older, page-reload-based forum software.

The project traces back to a design philosophy Toby Zerner conceived around 2010 and prototyped by 2012, and it is commonly described as growing out of the lineage of two earlier PHP forum projects, FluxBB and esoTalk, reflecting the backgrounds of Zerner and FluxBB creator Franz Liedke, who both worked on the project in its early years. Flarum was open-sourced publicly in December 2014 after an initial Kickstarter campaign was canceled in favor of free distribution, and it reached its first stable 1.0.0 release on May 27, 2021, after an unusually long multi-year public beta period. Since 2019, the project has been led by Daniel Klabbers and is now legally owned by the nonprofit Flarum Foundation, registered in the Netherlands.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: At its core, Flarum provides discussions organized by tags rather than traditional nested subforums, role-based groups and permissions, a Markdown and WYSIWYG-capable post editor, and real-time-feeling navigation powered by its single-page frontend. Notifications, mentions, and subscriptions are handled through bundled first-party extensions rather than being hardwired into the core, which keeps the base installation lean.

Almost every additional capability, such as social login, private messaging, advanced moderation and audit logging, search improvements, SEO tooling, and integrations with external services, is added through Flarum's Composer-based extension system, which exposes a documented Extension API for PHP and JavaScript developers. Hundreds of free community extensions are available on GitHub and Packagist, alongside an official premium extensions marketplace where invited developers sell more advanced add-ons directly to forum owners.

long_form_blocks.heading: Technical Architecture
paragraphs: Flarum's backend is a PHP application that uses components from the Laravel framework and exposes a REST API that the frontend, and any external client, communicates with. The frontend was originally written in Ember.js when Flarum launched in 2015 but was later rewritten in Mithril.js, a smaller JavaScript framework, to keep the single-page interface lightweight.

Self-hosting Flarum requires a PHP-capable web server, a MySQL or MariaDB-compatible database, and Composer for installing the core and any extensions. The actively developed 2.x branch requires PHP 8.3 or newer as of 2026, while the long-lived 1.x branch, culminating in the 1.8.x series, remains compatible with PHP 7.3 and above and is still widely deployed on production forums that have not yet upgraded.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Flarum itself does not sell an official hosted or cloud product; an early plan to pair a paid hosting offering with the project's 2014 Kickstarter campaign was dropped within weeks in favor of pure open-source distribution. The core software remains free to download, self-host, and modify indefinitely, with no feature limits in the free tier.

Site owners who prefer not to manage their own infrastructure can use independent, third-party managed-hosting providers such as Elestio, 20i, TMDHosting, and 365i, which charge their own monthly fees for installation, updates, backups, and support. The only spending that flows directly into the Flarum ecosystem is optional: forum owners can purchase individual premium extensions, priced by their independent developers as one-off purchases or subscriptions, through Flarum's own marketplace.

long_form_blocks.heading: How Flarum Compares
paragraphs: Flarum's closest competitors are Discourse, phpBB, vBulletin, XenForo, and NodeBB. Discourse, built on Ruby on Rails, offers a similarly modern experience but is generally heavier to self-host and is most often consumed as a paid hosted service. phpBB is an older, more traditional PHP forum package with a full-page-reload interface and a large legacy extension library. vBulletin and XenForo are long-standing commercial, closed-source forum platforms sold under paid software licenses rather than distributed as open source. NodeBB takes a comparable single-page-application approach but is built on Node.js and MongoDB instead of PHP and MySQL.

Flarum differentiates itself through a fully free, MIT-licensed core, a lighter and faster interface than phpBB or Discourse tend to deliver on comparable hardware, and a minimalist, extension-first design philosophy that keeps the base installation small so site owners only add the complexity their community genuinely needs.

features.title: Composer-based extension system

features.title: Groups and permissions

features.title: Markdown and WYSIWYG editor

features.title: Notifications and email digests

features.title: Theming and customization
features.description: Administrators can adjust forum-wide styling, install community or premium themes, and apply custom CSS through the admin dashboard without touching core code.

features.title: REST API
features.description: A documented REST API exposes forum data and actions, enabling integrations, custom clients, and headless or embedded use cases beyond the default frontend.

pros: Core software is fully free and MIT-licensed with no feature paywalls, unlike commercial platforms such as vBulletin or XenForo.
Single-page application frontend built on Mithril.js feels noticeably faster and lighter than traditional full-page-reload forum software like classic phpBB.
Composer-based extension architecture and documented Extension API make it straightforward for developers to build and share custom functionality.
Actively governed by the nonprofit Flarum Foundation, giving the project institutional continuity independent of any single maintainer.
cons: Requires self-hosting knowledge, including PHP, Composer, and a MySQL or MariaDB database, since Flarum offers no official managed hosting of its own.
The project took an unusually long time to reach a stable 1.0 release, moving through roughly sixteen beta versions across nearly six years of public testing.
Some of the most advanced functionality is only available through premium, paid third-party extensions rather than being built into core.
The newest 2.x branch requires a relatively recent PHP 8.3 or higher runtime, which can require a server upgrade for site owners on older shared hosting plans.

faqs.question: Is Flarum free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. Flarum's core software is free and open source under the MIT license, and it can be downloaded, self-hosted, and modified at no cost. Some advanced functionality is available only through optional paid premium extensions sold by independent developers on Flarum's marketplace.

faqs.question: Does Flarum offer its own hosted or cloud version?
faqs.answer: No. Flarum does not operate an official paid hosting service; an early plan to pair a hosted offering with its 2014 Kickstarter campaign was dropped in favor of pure open-source distribution. Site owners who do not want to self-host can use independent third-party managed-hosting providers such as Elestio, 20i, TMDHosting, or 365i.

faqs.question: What technology does Flarum use?
faqs.answer: Flarum's backend is written in PHP using components from the Laravel framework, and its frontend is a single-page application built with the Mithril.js JavaScript framework. Extensions and themes are distributed and installed as Composer packages.

faqs.question: Who created Flarum and when?
faqs.answer: Toby Zerner conceived Flarum's design philosophy around 2010 and built early prototypes by 2012. The project was publicly open-sourced in December 2014 after Zerner canceled a Kickstarter campaign, and Franz Liedke, creator of the earlier FluxBB forum software, joined the effort in March 2015. Flarum reached its first stable 1.0.0 release on May 27, 2021.

faqs.question: How does Flarum compare to Discourse and phpBB?
faqs.answer: Compared to Discourse, Flarum is generally lighter to self-host and does not push users toward a paid hosted plan. Compared to phpBB, Flarum offers a more modern single-page interface instead of full-page reloads. Both Discourse and phpBB have larger, longer-established extension and plugin ecosystems in certain areas.

faqs.question: Who governs the Flarum project today?
faqs.answer: Flarum is led by Daniel Klabbers and is legally owned by the Flarum Foundation, a nonprofit entity (registered in the Netherlands as Stichting Flarum) established after original creator Toby Zerner stepped back from the project in July 2019 to pursue a separate premium forum software venture.

use_cases.title: Customer support and peer-to-peer help communities
use_cases.description: Companies use Flarum to run self-service support forums where customers can ask questions, share solutions, and reduce direct support ticket volume, as demonstrated by mobile network giffgaff's large peer-support community.
use_cases.audience: Support and customer success teams at product and telecom companies

use_cases.title: Niche hobby and interest communities
use_cases.description: Flarum's lightweight footprint and free MIT license make it accessible for independent site owners and hobbyists building topic-focused discussion communities without ongoing licensing costs.
use_cases.audience: Independent community builders and hobbyist site owners

use_cases.title: Developer and open-source project forums
use_cases.description: Open-source and developer-tool projects use Flarum's REST API and extension system to run community discussion boards that can be customized and integrated with existing developer workflows.
use_cases.audience: Open-source maintainers and developer relations teams
